Question.content: The Flow Meter Test rig (F1-21) accessory is designed to be positioned on the side of the hydraulics bench _______ the P channel.

Explanation.content:
🔧 F1-21 Flow Meter Test Rig Placement

The F1-21 is a modular accessory designed to sit alongside the hydraulics bench, connecting to the P-channel (the pump outlet).

📌 Why "alongside"?
  • The bench provides water supply and return.
  • The test rig sits next to (alongside) the bench, connected by hoses.
  • This modular design lets you swap different experiment accessories easily.
💡 Visualize It: Like a printer sitting alongside a computer — connected but separate.

🎓 Answer: alongside

Question.content: The venturi meter, variable area meter and the orifice plate are installed in a _______ to permit direct comparison.

Explanation.content:
🔗 Installed in Series — Why?

All three flowmeters (Venturi, Rotameter, Orifice Plate) are placed one after another in the same pipe line. This is called being in series.

✅ Benefits of Series Installation:
  • Same flow rate passes through all meters simultaneously.
  • You can compare readings directly at the exact same flow condition.
  • No need to run multiple experiments — one test gives data for all three!
💡 Analogy: Like three different speedometers on the same car. At 60 km/h, you can see which one reads accurately and which one is off.

🎓 Answer: series

Question.content: Flow through the test section is regulated using a flow control valve. This together with the bench control valve permits variation of the system _______ pressure.

Explanation.content:
🎛️ Controlling Static Pressure

There are two valves working together:

  • Bench control valve — controls water from the pump (like a main tap).
  • Flow control valve — fine-tunes flow through the test section.
📌 What is Static Pressure?
Static pressure is the pressure you'd measure if the fluid were still (not moving). By adjusting both valves, you can change the overall system pressure while keeping flow steady. This lets you study how pressure affects flowmeter performance.
💡 Simple Analogy: Think of the bench valve as the "main water supply" and the flow control valve as the "shower head dial." Together, they control both flow rate and the pressure you feel.

🎓 Answer: static

Question.content: Pressure tapings in the circuit are connected to an eight-bank manometer which incorporates an air inlet/outlet valve in the top manifold with facilities to connect a _______ pump.

Explanation.content:
💨 The Eight-Bank Manometer & Hand Pump

A hand pump is used to adjust the air pressure inside the manometer manifold. This sets the water levels to a convenient starting position before taking readings.

🔧 Why a Hand Pump?
  • It lets you zero the manometer — set all water columns to the same level.
  • You can apply slight pressure to prevent water from being sucked too high or too low.
  • It's manual (hand-operated), so no electricity needed — simple and reliable.
💡 Visualize: Like using a rubber bulb to prime a siphon or set a water level in a U-tube.

🎓 Answer: hand

Question.content: The pressure tapings for the manometers are arranged to give a set of readings _______ the flow meters in the system.

Explanation.content:
📍 Pressure Tapings "Around" the Flowmeters

The pressure tapings (small holes in the pipe wall) are positioned before, at, and after each flowmeter. This allows you to measure pressure changes around (upstream, throat, downstream) each device.

📌 For Example — Venturi Meter:
  • Upstream tapping — before the meter (high pressure)
  • Throat tapping — at the narrowest section (low pressure)
  • Downstream tapping — after the meter (recovering pressure)

By connecting these to the manometer, you see the pressure profile around the meter.

💡 Why "Around"? It means you're capturing the complete pressure story — not just one point, but the entire region where the flow is changing.

🎓 Answer: around

Question.content: The objective of flow meter demonstration experiment is to investigate the operation and characteristics of three different basic types of volumetric measuring _______ of energy losses.

Explanation.content:
🎯 What We're Investigating

The full objective: to investigate the operation, characteristics, and accuracies of three different flowmeters, along with the energy losses they cause.

📊 What "Accuracies" Means Here:
  • How close does each meter's reading come to the true flow rate?
  • Which meter is most accurate at low flow? At high flow?
  • How do energy losses (pressure drops) affect accuracy?
💡 Big Picture: You're not just learning how each meter works. You're learning which meter to trust for different jobs based on accuracy and energy efficiency.

🎓 Answer: accuracies

Question.content: The hydraulics bench allows us to measure _______ by timed volume collection.

Explanation.content:
⏱️ The "Bucket & Stopwatch" Method

The hydraulics bench has a built-in volumetric tank (like a large measuring cylinder). You direct water into it, time how long it takes to fill a known volume, and calculate flow rate.

📐 The Formula: \[ Q = \frac{\text{Volume}}{\text{Time}} \]
  • If you collect 10 liters in 20 seconds, flow rate = 10 L ÷ 20 s = 0.5 L/s
💡 Why This Matters: This is your reference measurement — the "truth" against which you compare the Venturi, orifice, and rotameter readings. It's simple but highly accurate.

🎓 Answer: flow rate (also called discharge — see Question 12!)

Question.content: Discharge coefficient values can be determined from measurements of the height of the free surface of water above the notch base, and the corresponding _______ flow rate.

Explanation.content:
📏 Finding the Discharge Coefficient (Cd)

For a weir (like a V-notch or rectangular notch), the discharge coefficient is a correction factor that accounts for real-world effects like friction and contraction.

🧪 How to Find Cd:
  1. Measure the water height (H) above the notch base.
  2. Measure the actual volume flow rate (Q_actual) using the bench's volumetric tank.
  3. Calculate the theoretical flow rate (Q_theory) using the weir formula.
  4. Cd = Q_actual ÷ Q_theory
💡 Real-World Insight: Cd is rarely 1.0. It's usually between 0.6 and 0.95 depending on the weir shape. Finding it helps you use the weir as an accurate flow measuring device.

🎓 Answer: Volume

Question.content: To determine the characteristics of open-channel flow is the objective of _______ experiment.

Explanation.content:
🌊 Flume Weirs Experiment

While the flowmeter experiment deals with pipe flow (closed conduit), the flume weirs experiment deals with open channel flow — water flowing in a channel with a free surface (like a river or irrigation ditch).

🔍 What You Learn:
  • How water behaves when it flows over a notch or weir.
  • Relationship between water height and flow rate.
  • Energy losses in open channels.
  • How to design spillways, dams, and irrigation structures.
💡 Connection: In the hydraulics lab, you'll do both experiments. Question 9 is reminding you which experiment is for open channels (Flume Weirs) vs pipes (Flow Meter Test Rig).

🎓 Answer: Flume Weirs

Question.content: The _______ is used for flow meter demonstration experiment.

Explanation.content:
🔬 The Meter Test Apparatus

This is the formal name for the equipment that holds the three flowmeters (Venturi, orifice, rotameter) and the manometers. It's often called the F1-21 Flow Meter Test Rig.

📌 What It Includes:
  • Three flowmeters installed in series
  • Pressure tapings for each meter
  • Connection to an eight-bank manometer
  • Flow control valve
  • Mounting that attaches to the hydraulics bench
💡 Name Recognition: In your lab manual, it's called the "Meter Test Apparatus" or "Flow Meter Test Rig." Knowing the equipment name is important for writing lab reports!

🎓 Answer: meter test apparatus

Question.content: Variable area meter, venturi meter and orifice plate are all referred to as _______ meters.

Explanation.content:
⚠️ Important Terminology Note

This question points out that these three devices are collectively called Flume Meters in your lab context. However, be careful — this is specific to your equipment manual.

📚 Clarification:
  • In general engineering terms, these are called flowmeters or flow measuring devices.
  • In your F1-21 manual, they are grouped under "Flume Meters" because they're all mounted on a common test rig.
  • Don't confuse this with a "flume" (open channel) — here it's just a naming convention for the experiment.
💡 Exam Tip: When answering questions from your lab manual, use the terms exactly as they appear in the manual. Here, that term is "Flume Meters."

🎓 Answer: Flume

Question.content: Flow rate can also be known as _______.

Explanation.content:
📖 Flow Rate = Discharge

In fluid mechanics, flow rate and discharge mean the same thing: the volume of fluid passing a point per unit time.

📐 Notation: \[ Q = \frac{V}{t} \]
  • Q = flow rate / discharge (m³/s, L/s, etc.)
  • V = volume (m³, L)
  • t = time (seconds)
💡 Common Usage:
  • "The river's discharge is 500 m³/s."
  • "The pump's flow rate is 50 L/min."
Both sentences mean the same thing — volume per time.

🎓 Answer: Discharge

Question.content: Variable area flume meter is also referred to as _______.

Explanation.content:
🔄 Rotameter = Variable Area Meter

The variable area meter is commonly called a rotameter. It's the one with a tapered tube and a float that rises as flow increases.

⚙️ How It Works:
  • Fluid flows upward through a tapered tube.
  • A float sits inside; higher flow pushes the float higher.
  • The annular area around the float changes with position — hence "variable area."
  • You read the flow rate directly from a scale next to the float's position.
💡 Why "Rotameter"? The float often spins (rotates) slightly during operation, giving it the name. It's a simple, direct-reading flowmeter — no calibration curve needed for many applications.

🎓 Answer: rotameter

Question.content: A quadrant is to the Hydraulic Pressure experiment, just as a venturimeter is to the _______ experiment.

Explanation.content:
🔄 Analogy: Quadrant ↔ Venturi

This is asking you to recognize which experiment each piece of equipment belongs to:

Equipment Experiment
Quadrant (Hydrostatic Pressure apparatus) Hydraulic Pressure experiment
Venturimeter Meter Demands / Flow Meter experiment
💡 Understanding the Phrase "Meter Demands":
"Meter Demands" refers to the experiment where you study how different flowmeters (Venturi, orifice, rotameter) perform. The venturimeter is the main device in that experiment, just as the quadrant is the main device in the hydraulic pressure experiment.

🎓 Answer: Meter Demands

Question.content: Partially submerged vertical plane surface is mathematically expressed as F = _______.

Explanation.content:
📐 Hydrostatic Force Formula

The force on a partially submerged vertical plane surface is given by:

F = ρ g A h̅

or in the notation used: F = P g A h

🔤 What Each Symbol Means:
  • F = Total hydrostatic force (Newtons)
  • P (or ρ) = Density of the fluid (kg/m³)
  • g = Acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)
  • A = Area of the submerged surface (m²)
  • h (or h̅) = Depth to the centroid of the submerged area (m)
💡 Intuition: This formula says that the force pushing on a submerged wall depends on:
  • How heavy the fluid is (ρg)
  • How big the wall is (A)
  • How deep the middle of the wall is (h)
The deeper you go, the greater the force — which is why dams are thicker at the bottom!

🎓 Answer: Pg A h
